section.title: Deliver Me, O Lord
section.sectionRationale: The first psalm to carry a historical superscription (during Absalom's rebellion), and the model lament of the Psalter: a cry of distress that moves through confidence to sleep in peace and a closing benediction over God's people.
section.thesis: Surrounded by many enemies, David finds that the LORD is a shield around him, so that he can lie down and sleep even under threat, and he prays for deliverance while confessing that salvation belongs to the LORD alone.
section.frameNote: Four short movements marked by Selah: the crisis of many foes (vv1-2), confidence in the LORD as shield and sustainer (vv3-6), a prayer for deliverance (v7), and the closing confession that salvation and blessing belong to the LORD (v8).

units.label: Many foes have risen
units.note: God will not deliver him, they say

verses.text: O LORD, how my foes have increased! How many rise up against me!
Many say of me, "God will not deliver him." {{selah|Selah}}

units.label: The LORD is my shield
units.note: I lie down and sleep, for the LORD sustains me

verses.text: But You, O LORD, are a shield around me, my glory, and the One who lifts my head.
To the LORD I cry aloud, and He answers me from His holy mountain. {{selah|Selah}}
I lie down and sleep; I wake again, for the LORD sustains me.
I will not fear the myriads set against me on every side.
Arise, O LORD! Save me, O my God! Strike all my enemies on the jaw; break the teeth of the wicked.
Salvation belongs to the LORD; may Your blessing be on Your people. {{selah|Selah}}

selah.gloss: A musical or liturgical marker of uncertain exact meaning, likely calling for a pause, a lifting of voices, or an instrumental interlude. Here it punctuates the psalm's turn from complaint to confidence and from confidence to closing praise, inviting the singer to pause and let each truth settle.

basic.context: Psalm 3 carries the Psalter's first historical superscription: a psalm of David when he fled from his son Absalom. Surrounded by enemies who say God will not save him, David declares the LORD his shield, sleeps in peace under threat, and prays for deliverance, closing with the confession that salvation belongs to the LORD alone.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a lament, a genuine cry of distress addressed to God, not a private complaint hidden from Him; the Psalms teach that pouring out real fear and asking for God to strike enemies is honest, Spirit-shaped prayer, not sub-Christian venting, though the New Testament reframes personal vengeance as belonging to God alone, Romans 12:19.
basic.originalLanguages: Three times the psalm is marked by selah, a pause inviting reflection, at the turn from complaint to confidence, from confidence to prayer, and at the closing confession. The image of God as a shield around David and the one who lifts his head pictures both protection and restored dignity.
basic.typology: David, betrayed by his own son and hunted by those closest to him, foreshadows Christ, betrayed by Judas and abandoned by His own, who also entrusted Himself to the Father and could sleep, even the sleep of death, in perfect confidence of vindication.
basic.christ: Jesus prayed psalms like this one from the cross, facing enemies who mocked, 'He trusts in God, let God deliver Him' echoes the taunt David faced; Christ's own resurrection is the ultimate answer to David's cry that salvation belongs to the LORD.
basic.god: God is pictured here as shield, glory, and the one who lifts the bowed head, an intensely personal defender who answers prayer from His holy mountain even while enemies multiply on every side.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 15-17 narrates Absalom's rebellion behind this psalm. Matthew 27:43 echoes the taunt that God will not deliver the sufferer. Romans 12:19 reframes vengeance as belonging to God rather than to us.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 3 teaches every hunted, betrayed believer to bring real fear honestly to God, to rest in Him as a shield even under threat, and to trust that salvation belongs to the LORD, a confession fulfilled completely when Jesus, betrayed and surrounded by enemies, rose to become salvation itself for His people.

context.literary: A model individual lament, moving through the classic pattern of complaint, confidence, petition, and praise, marked by three uses of selah that punctuate its emotional turns.
context.historical: The superscription sets this psalm during Absalom's rebellion, 2 Samuel 15-18, when David fled Jerusalem betrayed by his own son and pursued by a large following, giving vivid, personal weight to the psalm's talk of foes who have increased.
context.canonical: As the first psalm with a historical superscription, Psalm 3 anchors the Psalter's laments in real, lived crisis, teaching that honest distress and confident faith belong together in prayer, a pattern repeated throughout the book.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice honest fear before overwhelming odds while modeling how confidence in God as shield and deliverer can coexist with, rather than replace, urgent petition for rescue.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific crisis during Absalom's revolt; it prescribes for every believer the pattern of honest lament followed by confident trust, not a guarantee of the exact same circumstances or timeline of deliverance.

debates.question: Is the prayer 'strike all my enemies on the jaw, break the teeth of the wicked' appropriate for Christians to pray today?

views.label: A legitimate cry for God's justice, not personal vengeance
views.case: David appeals to God, not to his own hand, asking the righteous Judge to act; the image pictures rendering an enemy harmless rather than personal cruelty, and Scripture elsewhere commends crying out for God's justice, Revelation 6:10.

views.label: An Old Testament expression tempered by New Testament teaching on loving enemies
views.case: Jesus commands love of enemies and prayer for persecutors, Matthew 5:44, so Christians should pray such imprecations, if at all, as appeals for justice and restraint of evil rather than personal desire for an enemy's harm.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this is prayer directed to God for justice, not license for personal revenge; Christians can pray for God's justice against evil while also praying, as Christ taught, for their enemies' repentance.

study.originalLanguages: David's threefold selah punctuates the psalm's emotional turns, inviting the singer to pause at each shift from fear to confidence to praise. He calls the LORD a magen, shield, encircling him on every side, and the one who lifts his kavod, glory or head, restoring the dignity his enemies tried to strip away.

typology.type: The betrayed king who sleeps in peace under threat, Psalm 3:5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, betrayed by His own and surrounded by enemies, who entrusted Himself to the Father even to the sleep of death, confident of resurrection.

study.christ: David, betrayed by his own son Absalom and mocked with the taunt that God would not save him, foreshadows Jesus, betrayed by Judas and mocked at the cross with almost the identical words, He trusts in God, let God rescue Him now, Matthew 27:43. Where David could sleep in confident hope, Christ went further, entrusting Himself even to death, and the resurrection is God's final answer that salvation belongs to the LORD.
study.god: God is shield, glory, and the lifter of the bowed head, a defender who answers from His holy mountain even while enemies multiply. He is not distant from the crisis; He is described in the most personal, protective terms a hunted man could reach for.

crossReferences.note: Narrates Absalom's rebellion, the historical crisis named in this psalm's superscription.
Echoes the taunt 'God will not deliver him' hurled at David, now hurled at Christ on the cross.
Reframes the desire for an enemy's judgment as belonging to God's vengeance, not our own hand.

study.oneStory: Psalm 3 teaches every believer under threat to bring real fear honestly before God, to rest as under a shield even while surrounded, and to confess that salvation belongs to the LORD alone. Jesus, betrayed and mocked with David's own taunt, is that salvation in person, risen to become the blessing this psalm asks upon God's people.

sermon.title: Praying Our Fears
sermon.why: Keller works through Psalm 3, showing how David, with real armies pursuing him, learns to pray his fear rather than be paralyzed by it, tracing the four steps out of the pit in verses 3-8.

quiz.q: What is the crisis David describes at the start of Psalm 3?

options.text: Many foes have risen against him, and many say God will not deliver him.
options.note: This is the psalm's opening complaint, both the number of enemies and their taunt about God.

options.text: He is suffering from a physical illness.
options.note: The psalm's crisis is enemies and betrayal, tied to Absalom's rebellion, not illness.

options.text: He has lost his kingdom to a foreign power.
options.note: The crisis is closer to home, a rebellion led by his own son, not a foreign conquest.

options.text: He has no crisis; the psalm is purely a hymn of praise.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explicitly describe a crisis of many foes and a taunt against his faith.

quiz.q: How does David describe the LORD in verse 3?

options.text: A shield around him, his glory, and the One who lifts his head.
options.note: This threefold description pictures protection and restored dignity.

options.text: A distant king who has not yet responded.
options.note: David describes God as intimately present and protective, not distant or unresponsive.

options.text: A silent witness to his suffering.
options.note: The psalm describes active protection and answered prayer, not mere silent observation.

options.text: An angry judge with no mercy.
options.note: Verse 3 is entirely about protection, glory, and lifted dignity, not wrath.

quiz.q: What surprising thing is David able to do in verse 5, despite being surrounded by enemies?

options.text: Lie down and sleep, and wake again, because the LORD sustains him.
options.note: This is the psalm's striking picture of peace under threat, resting in God's sustaining care.

options.text: Organize a counterattack against his enemies.
options.note: The psalm describes rest and trust in God's care, not a military strategy.

options.text: Flee the country entirely.
options.note: While David has fled Jerusalem historically, the verse itself describes sleeping peacefully, not further flight.

options.text: Give up hope entirely.
options.note: The verse is the opposite of despair; it is a picture of confident rest.

quiz.q: What does David confess in the closing verse of the psalm?

options.text: Salvation belongs to the LORD, and he prays for the LORD's blessing on His people.
options.note: This is the psalm's climactic confession and closing prayer.

options.text: He will save himself by his own strength.
options.note: The psalm explicitly credits salvation to the LORD, not to David's own strength.

options.text: His enemies will never be defeated.
options.note: The psalm's tone is confident hope in God's deliverance, not resignation to defeat.

options.text: The psalm ends without any resolution.
options.note: Verse 8 gives a clear, confident closing confession and blessing.

quiz.q: How does this psalm anticipate Christ?

options.text: David, betrayed by his own son and mocked that God would not save him, foreshadows Jesus, betrayed by Judas and mocked with nearly the same taunt at the cross.
options.note: Matthew 27:43 echoes almost the exact accusation David faced here.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ's life or the New Testament at all.
options.note: Actually the taunt in Matthew 27:43 draws directly on this kind of mockery.

options.text: It only describes generic suffering with no specific New Testament echo.
options.note: The connection is specific: the same accusation, that God will not deliver the sufferer, appears at the cross.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Christ's crucifixion.
options.note: The psalm draws a thematic and verbal connection, not a chronological prediction.

questions: David brings his real fear, many foes, and a real taunt about his faith, honestly to God rather than hiding it. What fear or doubt are you tempted to hide from God rather than bring to Him in prayer?

David can sleep peacefully even while surrounded by enemies, because the LORD sustains him. What is currently keeping you awake with worry, and what would it look like to entrust that specific fear to God tonight?

David asks God, not himself, to deal with his enemies, appealing to God's justice rather than taking revenge into his own hands. Where do you need to release a desire for personal vengeance and instead trust God's justice?

The psalm closes with salvation belonging to the LORD alone, not to David's own effort or cleverness. Where are you tempted to trust your own strategies for rescue rather than resting in what God alone can do?

Jesus faced betrayal and the same mocking taunt David did, yet trusted the Father completely, even to death. How does knowing that Christ walked this exact path of betrayal and mockery change how you carry your own experiences of betrayal?

translationNotes.note: Selah appears here for the first time in the Psalter; its precise meaning is debated, but it likely functioned as a musical or liturgical pause, inviting reflection at a turning point in the psalm.
The One who lifts my head can describe restoring someone from shame or defeat to honor and confidence, a vivid picture of God reversing the humiliation David's enemies intended for him.

Strike all my enemies on the jaw, break the teeth of the wicked uses animal imagery, picturing enemies as beasts rendered harmless by broken teeth, an appeal for God's justice rather than a description of personal cruelty.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 3:1-8. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. Sermon link verified this session by fetching the Gospel in Life sermon page for Psalm 3.
